speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, interview with Dr. Paul Parks, October 23rd, 1995. Did you have any connection or knowledge of the Haganah, or the Israeli-- at that time it wasn't Israel-- but the Bricha or Haganah helping in these situations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the only thing I knew-- and this has been years later, when I went to Israel. Harold Katz was a good friend of mine, was a part of the Haganah. And he was also-- he was in the-- he was one of the people that-- one of the Israeli heroes, I think. From the United States, he went to Israel. He was a part of that. And he was a part of the period when the British were there, the mandate.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was fighting against the mandate. So he was the only person I knew. And he used to talk-- and he's a good friend of mine. But that's been lately. What I did meet when I was there, in Israel, the first time went-- or I was there-- I met a German-- I mean, an Israeli judge named Azula, who was in the Court at Haifa. It was a three-judge court at Haifa. And we became very close friends. And he was there during-- he was a judge during the mandate. And he was-- he explained to me many of the things he had been through. I think he and I had this long debate. Because I sat in on one of his trials. It was an elderly Arab fellow who had killed his wife. His wife was about 17. And he must have been 74. And he said he found her with another man and that when he found her with the other man, he had a right to kill her, and he did. Under Israeli law, it was that he's guilty of murder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Azoulai and I sat I sat up one night talking. I said, you know, I think it's important that somehow or another we understand other people's culture to the point where sometimes that tempers the-- the-- the justice that we give people. Because here is this old man, 74 years old, who's always lived in the world where that was perfectly legitimate to do. And suddenly, he's running into Western culture. And Israel is a Western nation in the Middle East. And it's Western by design. It's Western by desire. It's Western, OK? And again, since that's the case, there's no way. How can you severely discipline this man for an act which was a part of his culture? And we talked about it. And interestingly enough, the next day, about three or four days later, he was sentencing this man. And he gave him three months plus a period of some labor and let him go. And I said-- because he would never survive. Yeah, we just talked. And he said, are you satisfied? And I said, [LAUGHS] yes, I guess I am as satisfied as I can become. Because under your rules, you couldn't let him go. But you did understand what he was going through. Yeah, but you-- you-- you did not have any contact at all with Haganah, or any of that sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm also-- you have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever talked to them about your experiences, particularly the liberation of Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I hadn't for a long time. It's been just in the last five or six years that I did sit and talk to them about it. I didn't tell anybody about it. And it was because of several people who are friends of mine, who have-- who-- Arnie Soloway and some other people in the Jewish community said, I must come out and talk about it. It's been a little longer, about 10 years now. He said, you've gotta talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arnie Soloway. He said, you've gotta talk about it, and you've got to tell people what happened. And then there was another fellow who was head of the Jewish community council, who also said, you've got to tell us, Paul. There are people out here who are saying it didn't happen. You were an eyewitness to it happening. And I think you've got to sit up and say something about it, and tell people what happened. And so they got me engagements to talk to groups of people. And I've been doing it ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way have you been-- other than talking, whom have been talking to, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Interestingly enough, I guess I've talked to more-- more synagogue congregations than I have anybody else. However, I have talked to a veterans' group, as I told you, in [? Charleston ?] that-- and oh yes, I been-- I spoke in the-- in the-- in the program that they have in the high schools. What do they call it? There's a program.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's the one. So I've spoken. I've been on the speaking panel for that. And it's interestingly enough, also, every year I speak at-- once a year, I go up to Bentley College in their program. And they have a program on World War II. And I go up and I talk there. So I do that every year. And I've done that every year for the last 12 years maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other ways that this incident has impacted upon your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only thing I can say is it sensitized me about people. And that I can't-- I don't know of any group of people that I have a dislike for. I don't know any group of people that I don't care for. In fact, I'm writing an article now, which is going into the papers about Farrakhan. And I know the impact of Farrakhan. Because I was at the-- at the Million Man March. And what I'm really saying is, let's talk about why Farrakhan is the way he is. And let's talk about why I am the way I am and compare these two experiences in a sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: One is that Farrakhan came out of-- he-- he grew up in lower Roxbury, in a building owned by one of the most sleazy, awful landlord-- slum landlords that I have ever known, a fellow named Morris Gordon. Morris Gordon was his landlord, OK? Now the reason why I know this story is because Farrakhan's mother is a very close personal friend of my mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law and her family grew up in the same tenement. I grew up-- I-- when I came here, I moved into one of Morris Gordon's apartments in Roxbury, a building in Roxbury. The man is one of the worst sleazes I have ever met. He really is. He's just the pits. You just don't meet anybody worse than the Morris Gordon. And unfortunately, he was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: For instance, in my house I had-- had my children. And this man had a woman he was keeping up on the third floor, a black woman, that he was coming and spending two or three days with. OK? I mean, all those kind of things I find reprehensible about this man. But then I say in this article also that my experience was a lot different. Then I talk about my relationship and our family relationship with Gabe Segal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it says that you can't draw a conclusion about a whole race of people because of one person who is absolutely dissident and outrageous, that everybody brings along somebody outrageous. But the thing that Farrakhan has to try to get over is his feeling about Morris Gordon. Because that was the only Jew he knew. And you see, something about that-- the only Jew I knew was Gabe Siegel. And for years I saw three races. Until just recently I saw three races-- black, white, and Jews. When I was in the-- when I was in the civil rights movements, black, whites, and Jews. Those are the three races of mankind, [LAUGHS] as far as I was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sees Jews in a different kind of way because of the Morris Gordon experience. Now, I'm not trying to justify or trying to deal with that in any other way, but just say, look. Here are two comparisons. They are totally different. We walk away with different kinds of feelings. Let's sit down at the table and talk to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think liberating Dachau has some impact on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you're saying right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it definitely does. It definitely does. Because what I saw and what I buried-- and as far as I was concerned, it wasn't Jews. They were people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also have been involved in writing a-- is it writing a book or a film on black soldiers fighting on two fronts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- that was-- I didn't do that. That was done by a group out of New York, who-- who did the film. And also there was a book written about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your connection with that at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in the film?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They asked me to come down. I'm in the film.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- how did you feel about doing the film?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't really-- at first I didn't want to do it. Because I just didn't want to recall, or remember, or have to go through this again. But once again, several of my friends said, it's important that you do it. Because there's a need to have a balance. And I went in. And I did it. And-- and it turned out it was nominated for Academy Award. And it was a runner up for Academy Award.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you were fighting on two fronts as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I knew that. I knew that when I came back home, that was the other front. I had been there. You see, just being black in America-- at one point, I knew I never wanted to get married. I never wanted to have children. Because I didn't want to bring black children into a world they were going to be hated from the day they were born. And that was an awful, awful thing for me to overcome. I love my children. They're just very, very important to me. But I also worked very hard to try to see that they don't have to undergo what I underwent in terms of the bigotry and prejudices. Like when I was at Purdue, I used to come home every night. And there was a-- there was a-- a Quaker-- Quaker professor of mine who let me stay in his house in West Lafayette. Every night I came home, the kids would line both sides of the street and spit on me and call me a nigger-- all little kids, all the way to my-- to the front door. Those are the kinds of things that I don't want my children to have to go through. I remember taking my children down to Baltimore just before they removed the signs. I remember standing in front of the sign at the restroom, so they would never see a sign saying for black and for whites only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we end this tape, is there anything that-- and-- and show the memorabilia that you do have-- is there anything you-- we've left out or you want to add to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the only thing, I think, and that just came out yesterday. And I guess I'm still high on about the memorial that was just done. And I was a part of the committee that produced that. I just hope that somehow, through the words that we're saying here, and what other people are saying, and what Mr. Spielberg is doing, that it will act as a constant reminder to everybody that let's don't participate in bigotry and hate, and-- and making judgments about races and classes of people, based upon things which they can do nothing about. As my father used to say, never blame a man for what he can't do anything about. Because nobody can deal with the color of their skin. We can't change that. Although there are those who try. But then-- but those basic things about our eyes, the color of our hair, all these sorts of things, are things which we inherit. And there's nothing we can do about it. And we should not be blamed for that. And I'm hoping that Mr. Spielberg will use these interviews as ways to constantly say to people, think about it. Before you go down that road, think very seriously. Because the next person who goes-- maybe it will be you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very kindly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, surely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for sharing this whole history and your testimony and-- about both the war and Dachau and your own experiences with the Shoah, The Survivors of the Shoah Foundation. I do want us, before we end the tape completely, to show us the few things that you do have, and tell us the history behind them.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK to stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I nod my head, you can go right on.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we'll know your on tape. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a uniform. The only thing I have left in terms of a uniform is this jacket. And the only reason why that jacket survived was because of my mother. My mother captured that jacket from me when I came from the Army. Because I told her I was going to get rid of all the things that remind me of the Army. And she put it away. And she kept it until she died. And before she died, she made me promise that I wouldn't get rid of it. And I promised that I wouldn't. And here it is. The stripes on each should-- each arm is the-- is the stripes of a first sergeant. Now this jacket, remember, was given-- when I got this jacket I was in the Pacific. And they were issued to me just before I came back to the United States. So it's not the battle jacket that we wore in Europe. It's a dress uniform jacket that was given to us when we came back to the States from the Pacific.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what you see on my shoulders are-- are the patches that was given to us. They're not the patches we had when we were in Europe. The one over-- the one over-- on the-- on-- to my-- to my left is a patch that-- that came out of Army USAFI headquarters in Europe. And it identified-- it was given to us after we came back to the States. That's the one with the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the one with the star. This one on this side, with the arrow and the star and the stars, was given to us as a result of our participation in the war in the Pacific. It's a-- it's a Southwest Pacific patch, of the Army of the Southwest Pacific.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the little stripes on the left-- on the right shoulder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, those-- each one of those represents six months. And that represents the fact that I was overseas for two years, in Europe for two years. The-- the ribbons-- the little patch on the other side is-- is the Army discharge patch that we have, showing that you had an honorable discharge. On this side the star, the patch, the things that you see there are-- well, let's see. There's-- there's the Philippine Liberation ribbon, the World War II ribbon. The other one is-- is-- oh-- oh, shoot. I've forgotten the middle. And then the one down at the bottom and [INAUDIBLE], with the stars on it, is my combat ribbon from Europe, from the European-- European campaign. The stars on that-- each one represents a major campaign that I participated in-- Normandy, Northern France, Central Europe, and the-- the Rhineland and Central Europe. The next one, the yellow one, is the-- is the Philippine campaign ribbon, or the Southwest Pacific campaign ribbon. And the last one is a good conduct ribbon, a medal.

speaker: interviewer
text: The red with the white stripes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The red one's a good conduct medal. Now all of these are medals-- my-- my daughter had me-- she had asked me to get them. And she has all my medals now. I ordered them finally. She said she just wanted me to order my ribbon-- my medals, which I never had. And so I ordered them from the-- from the-- from the Defense Department and suddenly found that I don't-- I didn't have a record anymore of my participation in the war. Because all of our records were burned. My outfit's records all had burned in a major fire at the Army headquarters in St. Louis, where they kept the-- the records.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to send them a copy of my discharge to re-establish myself, so they would have that, at least, before I could get the ribbons. And they had-- and that's how I got my medals, rather. So that's the jacket. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me tell you what this is? OK. This is a picture of me when I was-- this is when I went into basic training in-- in Camp Lee, Virginia. This was the first time I got a chance to go on a weekend pass, a one-day pass, in fact. I went to town and had this picture made for my mother. And that was me in my uniform as a young recruit at Camp Lee, Virginia. I think I was somewhere around-- I guess I must have been 19. I keep saying this. I was 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a menorah. And there's a kind of great story about it. About 1970 or '71, somewhere in that range, our door-- someone knocked at my door. And-- and there was a person standing there with a menorah wrapped up in newspaper, saying he'd been trying to find me for an awful long time and that the person who made the menorah had died, and asked him to find me and give me the menorah as a gift. Because he was at Dachau, the person who made this. And it was made while he was in the prison camp at Dachau. And he wanted me to have it because I had met him there. I don't remember meeting him. I met several people that I talked to. And I don't know what he looks like. But this was given to me. And it's one of my priceless possessions and prized possessions. And I-- this is the first time I've really had it out of my house. But that's the story behind it. And when you look at it, it's made up out of-- and these over here, these areas look like concrete nails. And-- because I do a lot of construction-- you know, building. We develop and design buildings. So you see concrete nails. And then some of the rest of it, I just don't know what it is. But some of our greatest works of art have been done by people who took pieces of something and made something out of them. And the sensitiveness of the piece for a person who faced imminent death at any one point really seems to me it gives it a sense, and makes it something of very, very deep importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the person who-- there is a signature on there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a signature, which I can't read. [LAUGHS] I never read it. And I really don't know. I can't see it. I know it's signed. It's signed right here, by-- right here, by somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And it isn't exactly straight, either.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's a bit crooked. But, hey, the fact that he put it together at all, you know, under the conditions under which it was put together, that's of little consequence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the person who brought it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know the person who brought it to me. I ask him and he said, it's of little-- little importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me see if I can decipher the name for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tilt it forward so I can shoot it on camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll put it back. The name is Scheiberger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Scheiberger OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think you'll be able to see this, the name on the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't see it, no. I don't think you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. It's Scheiberger. And you-- you don't know who gave it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who gave it to me. The man gave it to me and he left immediately. But it's Scheiberger, OK. But that doesn't mean anything to me either, except it means that that's the person who did it. And maybe there's somebody in the world who knows who Scheiberger was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perhaps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you again, Dr. Parks, for sharing all of this very, very interesting information with us. And I wish you well.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been my privilege. And I hope that the program goes well. And I wish you folk the best. And maybe our paths will cross again one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hopefully.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
